topic: help writing a quote for a bathroom refit

turns.content: alright mate i need a hand with something. got a customer wants a full bathroom refit and i need to write up a proper quote for them. i usually just scribble numbers on a bit of paper but this ones a bigger job and i want it to look professional innit

Absolutely, a clean, professional-looking quote can make a big difference -- it builds trust and makes customers feel they're dealing with a proper business. Let's put something together for you.

To get started, can you tell me:

1. **What's the scope of work?** Are we talking rip-out and full refit? New suite (toilet, basin, bath or shower)? Tiling? Any plumbing rerouting?
2. **Rough size of the bathroom?**
3. **Is the customer supplying any materials, or are you supplying everything?**
4. **Any extras like underfloor heating, heated towel rail, extractor fan?**

Once I know what's involved, I can help you lay it out in a format that looks sharp.

yeah full rip out and refit. its a standard size bathroom maybe 2.5 by 2 metres. they want a walk in shower instead of a bath, new toilet, new basin with vanity unit. full wall tiling floor tiling. new towel radiator. im supplying all the labour obviously and theyre buying the suite themselves but im getting the tiles and all the fittings and bits

Nice one, that's a solid job. Here's a professional layout you could use. I'll break it into sections so the customer can see exactly what they're paying for:

---

**RM Plumbing & Heating**
**Quotation**

**Date:** [Date]
**Customer:** [Name]
**Address:** [Property address]
**Ref:** [Quote number]

---

**Scope of Work: Full Bathroom Refit**

| Item | Description | Cost |
|------|-------------|------|
| 1 | Strip out of existing bathroom suite, wall tiles, and floor tiles. Disposal of waste. | £___ |
| 2 | Supply & install walk-in shower enclosure inc. tray, screen, thermostatic mixer valve, and riser kit | £___ |
| 3 | Install customer-supplied WC | £___ |
| 4 | Install customer-supplied basin & vanity unit, inc. taps and waste | £___ |
| 5 | Supply & fit wall tiles (approx. ___ sq m) inc. adhesive, grout, trims | £___ |
| 6 | Supply & fit floor tiles (approx. 5 sq m) inc. adhesive, grout | £___ |
| 7 | Supply & fit chrome heated towel radiator inc. valves | £___ |
| 8 | All associated plumbing, pipework modifications & connections | £___ |
| 9 | Silicone sealing, finishing & clean-up | £___ |

**Total (exc. VAT):** £___

---

Do you want to plug in some rough numbers? What are you thinking for labour days and materials cost?

thats brilliant mate exactly what i needed. ok so labour wise im thinking 5 days on it. i charge 250 a day so thats 1250 for labour. tiles and adhesive and grout and trims ill say about 400. towel rad with valves about 120. shower tray screen and mixer maybe 450 if i get it from my trade account. all the fittings waste pipes copper bits silicone maybe 80. skip hire for the rip out 250. so all in im looking at about 2550?

That all sounds reasonable. Let me tally that up:

- **Labour (5 days @ £250/day):** £1,250
- **Tiles, adhesive, grout, trims:** £400
- **Heated towel radiator + valves:** £120
- **Shower tray, screen & mixer valve:** £450
- **Fittings, pipework, waste, silicone:** £80
- **Skip hire:** £250

**Total: £2,550**

That looks about right. A couple of thoughts:

1. **Contingency:** You might want to add a small contingency line (say 5-10%) in case you find any surprises behind the old tiles -- rotten joists, dodgy pipework, that sort of thing. You could word it as "Any additional work discovered during strip-out will be quoted separately" instead of adding a fixed amount.

2. **Payment terms:** Worth putting on there -- e.g., "30% deposit on acceptance, balance on completion" or however you normally do it.

3. **Validity:** Standard is "This quote is valid for 30 days from the date above."

Want me to write the whole thing out as finished text you can copy straight into a Word doc or email?

yes please mate write the whole thing out ready to go. and yeah good shout on the contingency thing ill put that note about additional work being quoted separately. payment terms ill do 30 percent deposit and balance on completion. and yeah 30 days validity sorted

Here you go -- ready to copy and paste:

---

**RM Plumbing & Heating**
[Your address]
[Your phone number]
[Your email]
Gas Safe Reg. No: [Your number]

---

**QUOTATION**

**Date:** [Date]
**Quote Ref:** RM-[number]
**Customer:** [Customer name]
**Property Address:** [Address]

---

**Scope of Work: Full Bathroom Refit**

Strip-out of existing bathroom suite, wall and floor tiles, and disposal of all waste via skip. Supply and installation of new bathroom as detailed below.

| # | Description | Cost |
|---|-------------|------|
| 1 | Full strip-out of existing suite, tiles, and fittings. Skip hire and waste disposal. | £250.00 |
| 2 | Supply & install walk-in shower — tray, glass screen, thermostatic mixer valve & riser kit | £450.00 |
| 3 | Installation of customer-supplied WC, inc. all connections | Inc. in labour |
| 4 | Installation of customer-supplied basin & vanity unit, inc. taps, trap & waste | Inc. in labour |
| 5 | Supply & fit wall tiles, inc. adhesive, grout & edge trims | £400.00 |
| 6 | Supply & fit floor tiles, inc. adhesive & grout (approx. 5 sq m) | Inc. above |
| 7 | Supply & fit chrome heated towel radiator, inc. valves | £120.00 |
| 8 | All associated plumbing — pipework modifications, connections, fittings & sundries | £80.00 |
| 9 | Labour — 5 days | £1,250.00 |

| | **Total** | **£2,550.00** |

*Note: This quote is based on the current condition of the bathroom as inspected. Any additional work arising from unforeseen issues discovered during strip-out (e.g. damaged joists, concealed pipework) will be discussed with you and quoted separately before proceeding.*

---

**Payment Terms:**
- 30% deposit (£765.00) payable on acceptance of this quote.
- Remaining balance (£1,785.00) payable on completion.

**Quote Validity:** This quotation is valid for 30 days from the date above.

**Accepted by:** ______________________ **Date:** ______________

---

You can drop your actual details into the brackets at the top. Anything you'd want to change?

mate thats absolutely spot on. proper professional that is. the customer is going to think ive got a secretary or something haha. one thing though should i put my gas safe number on there even though its a bathroom job not gas work?

Ha! No harm in looking like you've got a proper operation behind you.

And yes, I'd keep the Gas Safe number on there. Here's why:

1. **It shows credibility** -- even though this job doesn't involve gas, it tells the customer you're qualified, registered, and legitimate. It's like a trust badge.
2. **If there's a gas supply in the bathroom** (some older houses have a gas point for a bayonet heater or back boiler), you're covered to deal with it if it comes up during the strip-out.
3. **Future work** -- this customer might need a boiler job down the line. If your Gas Safe number is already on their paperwork, they know you do that too.

It's basically free advertising for your full skill set. Keep it on there.
yeah good point actually the more professional it looks the better innit. one more thing do you reckon i should put an estimated timeline on there? like how many days the job will take?

Definitely worth including. Customers always want to know how long their bathroom will be out of action. You said five days of labour, but factor in a realistic buffer -- things always take a bit longer than you plan. I'd put something like:

**Estimated duration: 5-7 working days**

That way you've set expectations honestly. If you finish in five days you look efficient, and if it stretches to six or seven they're not surprised. You could also add a line like "Start date to be mutually agreed" so you're not locking yourself into a specific date before you know your diary.

sorted thats going on there. 5 to 7 days sounds about right cos theres always something that takes longer than you think with a bathroom. tiling always eats up time if you want it done proper. do you reckon I should email it or print it off and hand it to them

Honestly, do both. Email them a PDF so they've got a digital copy they can refer to, and if you're going round to see them anyway, hand over a printed version too. Some people prefer holding paper, especially older customers. The email gives you a timestamp as well, which is useful if there's ever a dispute about what was quoted. Most word processors let you export as PDF -- just save it and attach it to a quick email saying "Hi [name], please find attached the quotation we discussed. Happy to answer any questions."

yeah good shout doing both. this customer is a bit old school actually so he'll appreciate the paper copy. right im sending this over tonight. cheers for that mate absolute lifesaver innit. proper professional looking quote now

Ha, glad to help! A well-presented quote goes a long way -- it sets you apart from the blokes who scribble on the back of a receipt. £2,550 for a full refit is very competitive too so I reckon you'll get the job. Good luck with it!
